@graph.synopsis: A wild, jungle road. It's raining heavily. Through the darkness,
@graph.scriptText:
1   EXT. JUNGLE - NIGHT

    A wild, jungle road. It's raining heavily. Through the darkness,
    we can barely make out a convoy of vehicles moving along the road.
    There are JEEPS, a BULLDOZER, and several large, equipment-laden
    TRUCKS. The convoy moves through the mud toward a large holding
    pen. It's surrounded by high fences, alive with electricity and
    blazing lights.

    Through the gate of the pen, we can just make out the silhouette
    of a large BOX — dark metal, reinforced. On the side: IN-GEN
    CORPORATION.

    WORKERS struggle to maneuver the box into position alongside the
    pen. MULDOON, the game warden, stands on top of the box.

    MULDOON (40s, British, when Muldoon talks, you listen) speaks
    into his radio.

                        MULDOON
              Okay, rotate!

    The workers begin to push the box, trying to line it up with the
    gates of the holding pen. MULDOON watches, tense.

                        MULDOON (CONT'D)
              Steady! Steady!

    A WORKER runs to push the box. He's right at the edge of the gate,
    too close. The box suddenly shudders. A sound comes from inside —
    a horrible scratching, a hissing —

                        WORKER
              Shoot her! Shoot her!

    The BOX lurches. The worker's leg goes through the gate — he's
    caught. He screams. Muldoon shouts —

                        MULDOON
              Stand back, stand back!

    Muldoon and the workers struggle with the gate. An ARM reaches
    out from the darkness inside the box, snatches the WORKER by the
    arm and drags him in. His terrified face disappears into the
    darkness. The SOUND of the raptor is horrible. The other workers
    back away in terror.

    The box is still. The jungle is quiet again except for rain.

    Muldoon stares at the gate. He closes his eyes.
@graph.contextPackSummary: A wild, jungle road. It's raining heavily. Through the darkness,

@graph.synopsis: SUPER: MANO DE DIOS AMBER MINE. DOMINICAN REPUBLIC.
@graph.scriptText:
2   EXT. MOUNTAINSIDE - DAY

    SUPER: MANO DE DIOS AMBER MINE. DOMINICAN REPUBLIC.

    A mountainside dotted with workers. They dig and sift in the
    tropical heat. A pair of mud-caked boots come into frame, picking
    their way carefully down a hillside toward a cave entrance.

    DONALD GENNARO (40s, a lawyer) makes his way down the slope,
    mopping sweat from his forehead. JUANITO ROSTAGNO, the mine
    manager, meets him at the bottom.

                        ROSTAGNO
              Senor Gennaro. Welcome to Costa Rica.

                        GENNARO
              I appreciate you meeting me.

                        ROSTAGNO
              Of course. Of course. In-Gen wants us all
              to cooperate fully. Fully.

    They walk toward the cave entrance.

                        GENNARO
              You're losing two workers a year. Hammond
              said there'd be no problems.

                        ROSTAGNO
              And the accident on Isla Nublar. The worker
              that was killed —

                        GENNARO
              That's why I'm here. The investors are
              nervous, Rostagno. The accident could
              invalidate our rights and Hammond needs to
              let us send people down to conduct an
              inspection.

                        ROSTAGNO
              He won't like that.

                        GENNARO
              I don't care what he likes.
@graph.contextPackSummary: SUPER: MANO DE DIOS AMBER MINE. DOMINICAN REPUBLIC.

@graph.synopsis: They enter the mine shaft. Cool and dim after the blazing sun.
@graph.scriptText:
3   EXT. CAVE - DAY

    They enter the mine shaft. Cool and dim after the blazing sun.
    Workers pick at the amber-streaked walls.

    Rostagno holds up a CHUNK OF AMBER — about the size of a baseball,
    dark gold, translucent. Inside, perfectly preserved — a mosquito.

                        ROSTAGNO
              We found it this morning. It's perfect.
              Better than any previous sample.

    Gennaro looks at the mosquito carefully. He's barely containing
    his excitement.

                        GENNARO
              If this works the way Hammond thinks it will...

                        ROSTAGNO
              The income stream alone —

                        GENNARO
              Forget the income stream. Do you have any
              idea what we're actually talking about?

    He stares at the mosquito in the amber. A moment of wonder.
    Then the lawyer in him takes over again.

                        GENNARO
              I need to send a report by end of day.
              I'll need full access.

                        ROSTAGNO
              You'll have it.

    He hands Gennaro the amber. Gennaro turns it in the light.
@graph.contextPackSummary: They enter the mine shaft. Cool and dim after the blazing sun.

@graph.synopsis: SUPER: TWO YEARS LATER.
@graph.scriptText:
4   EXT. THE DIG - ESTABLISHING - DAY

    SUPER: TWO YEARS LATER.

    Aerial view of the Montana badlands. Barren, dusty, spectacular.
    Heat shimmers off the flats.
@graph.contextPackSummary: SUPER: TWO YEARS LATER.

@graph.synopsis: SUPER: SNAKEWATER, MONTANA.
@graph.scriptText:
5   EXT. THE DIG - DAY

    SUPER: SNAKEWATER, MONTANA.

    Heat shimmers. A dozen WORKERS dig on a low hillside where the
    ribbed bones of a sauropod protrude from the earth. Equipment,
    tents, a scattering of vehicles.

    DR. ALAN GRANT (mid-thirties, a ragged-looking guy with intense
    concentration) is crouched over a computer display. The screen
    shows a three-dimensional model of a velociraptor skeleton,
    rotating slowly.

    DR. ELLIE SATTLER (late twenties, athletic-looking; there's an
    impatience about Ellie) picks at the ground nearby with a small
    tool, brushing dust.

    A BOY — about twelve, cocky and unimpressed — wanders over and
    stares at Grant's screen.

                        BOY
              That's a six-foot turkey.

    Grant turns. Studies the kid.

                        GRANT
              Try to imagine yourself in the Cretaceous
              Period. You get your first look at this
              "six-foot turkey" as you enter a clearing.
              He moves like a bird, lightly, bobbing his
              head. And you keep still because you think
              that maybe his visual acuity is based on
              movement — like a T-Rex — he'll lose you
              if you don't move. But no. Not Velociraptor.
              You stare at him, and he just stares right
              back. And that's when the attack comes. Not
              from the front, but from the side —

    ANOTHER RAPTOR — a second one you didn't even know was there —
    strikes from your blind side.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              — from the other two raptors you didn't even
              know were there. Because Velociraptor's a
              pack hunter, you see. He uses coordinated
              attack patterns and he is out in force today.
              And he slashes at you with this —

    He holds up a fossilized raptor CLAW.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              — a six-inch retractable claw, like a razor,
              on the middle toe. He doesn't bother to bite
              your jugular like a lion. He slashes at you
              here, or here —

    He gestures at the boy's soft belly, his throat.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              — or maybe across the belly, spilling your
              intestines. The point is, you are alive when
              they start to eat you.

    The boy stares at him, wide-eyed. He's not so cocky now.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              So try to show a little respect.

    Grant hands the claw back to one of the workers and returns to
    his screen.

                        ELLIE
              That's great, Alan. Way to make friends.

                        GRANT
              It's not a six-foot turkey.

                        ELLIE
              (amused)
              No, it's not.

    One of the WORKERS calls out.

                        WORKER
              Doctor Grant! It's working! The computer
              scan is working!

    Grant springs up and hurries over.
@graph.contextPackSummary: SUPER: SNAKEWATER, MONTANA.

@graph.synopsis: The workers gather around a printout rolling from a portable
@graph.scriptText:
6   EXT. THE DIG - CONTINUOUS

    The workers gather around a printout rolling from a portable
    printer. Grant tears it off and studies it. His face splits into
    a grin.

                        GRANT
              The computer enhancement is clear. This is
              the raptor skeleton. A complete specimen.

                        ELLIE
              (looking over his shoulder)
              Beautiful.

                        GRANT
              We'd better check the site. If this is as
              complete as the printout suggests —

    In the distance: the sound of a HELICOPTER approaching.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The workers gather around a printout rolling from a portable

@graph.synopsis: Everyone looks up. A helicopter drops into view — a sleek,
@graph.scriptText:
7   EXT. THE DIG - MOMENTS LATER

    Everyone looks up. A helicopter drops into view — a sleek,
    expensive corporate machine bearing the IN-GEN CORPORATION logo.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Everyone looks up. A helicopter drops into view — a sleek,

@graph.synopsis: The helicopter lands in a swirl of dust. The rotors scatter papers,
@graph.scriptText:
8   EXT. BASE CAMP - DAY

    The helicopter lands in a swirl of dust. The rotors scatter papers,
    equipment. Everyone squints and turns away.

    Grant and Ellie exchange a look.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The helicopter lands in a swirl of dust. The rotors scatter papers,

@graph.synopsis: The helicopter door opens. JOHN HAMMOND steps out — seventy-ish,
@graph.scriptText:
9   EXT. BASE CAMP - CONTINUOUS

    The helicopter door opens. JOHN HAMMOND steps out — seventy-ish,
    sprightly as hell, with bright, shining eyes and a silver beard.
    He uses a walking cane with a top made from the clearest amber
    Grant has ever seen. He's dressed entirely in white. He looks
    around with delight.

                        HAMMOND
              Dr. Grant! Dr. Sattler! I can't tell you
              how delighted I am to meet you both.

    He takes Grant's hand in both of his and pumps it warmly.

                        GRANT
              (wary)
              Mr. Hammond.

                        HAMMOND
              John. Please, call me John. I've been looking
              forward to this for weeks.

    He's already turning to look at the dig site, beaming.

                        HAMMOND
              I see you've found your raptor.

                        GRANT
              Part of one. Maybe more.

                        HAMMOND
              How exciting! How absolutely exciting!
@graph.contextPackSummary: The helicopter door opens. JOHN HAMMOND steps out — seventy-ish,

@graph.synopsis: Hammond, Grant, and Ellie sit in the shade of a battered trailer.
@graph.scriptText:
10  EXT. TRAILER - DAY

    Hammond, Grant, and Ellie sit in the shade of a battered trailer.
    Hammond has produced a bottle of CHAMPAGNE from his bag and is
    pouring it into paper cups before being invited to.

                        HAMMOND
              I'm not a betting man, but if I were, I
              would bet that you are both going to have
              a wonderful surprise very soon.

                        GRANT
              We're, uh... we're not quite in a position
              for surprises right now, Mr. Hammond.

                        HAMMOND
              John. And I believe you are. You see, I'd
              like to offer each of you a research grant.
              A very generous research grant to fund your
              research here for the next three to five
              years, whatever you need.

    Grant and Ellie stare at him. This is not nothing.

                        HAMMOND (CONT'D)
              In exchange, I want you to spend one weekend
              at my park. As consultants. To see if it
              passes muster.

                        ELLIE
              What kind of park?

                        HAMMOND
              Well... why don't you see for yourselves?

    He hands them each an air ticket. They look. Their eyes widen.

                        GRANT
              Costa Rica?

                        HAMMOND
              Just for the weekend. And then back here.
              Spared no expense, Dr. Grant. Spared no
              expense.

    He pours more champagne without being asked.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Hammond, Grant, and Ellie sit in the shade of a battered trailer.

@graph.synopsis: SUPER: SAN JOSE, COSTA RICA.
@graph.scriptText:
11  EXT. CAFE - DAY

    SUPER: SAN JOSE, COSTA RICA.

    An outdoor cafe near the waterfront. Hot sun. DENNIS NEDRY (late
    thirties, a big guy with a constant smile that might be laughing
    with you or at you) sits at a table covered with papers and
    coffee cups. LEWIS DODGSON approaches — a careful man, wearing
    a large straw hat and looking almost comically conspicuous.

    Nedry spots him.

                        NEDRY
              Dodgson!

    Several people turn to look.

                        NEDRY (CONT'D)
              Dodgson! We've got Dodgson here!

    He waves. Dodgson flinches.

                        NEDRY (CONT'D)
              See? Nobody cares.

    Dodgson sits quickly, his back to the other diners.

                        DODGSON
              Do you have to do that?

                        NEDRY
              You're the one wearing the hat.

    Dodgson sets a briefcase on the table and opens it toward Nedry.
    Inside: money, in cash, banded neatly.

                        DODGSON
              Fifty thousand. On delivery, the other fifty.

    Nedry looks at the money without touching it.

                        NEDRY
              How do you plan to get the embryos off the
              island?

    Dodgson produces a BARBASOL SHAVING CREAM CAN and slides it
    across the table to Nedry.

                        DODGSON
              The bottom twists off. Six cans packed in
              cryo-cells — that'll keep them viable for
              thirty-six hours. You'll bring them to the
              dock east of the lagoon at fifteen hundred.

    Nedry examines the can. The bottom does indeed twist off.

                        NEDRY
              Which species are you most interested in?

                        DODGSON
              All of them. Every embryo from every species
              on the island. Packed in dry ice. And you
              can make sure the island has a tiny little
              security problem.

    Nedry smiles. It's not a nice smile.

                        NEDRY
              System'll go down for eighteen minutes.
              I've timed it. I'll be able to stall long
              enough to get to the dock.

                        DODGSON
              Eighteen minutes.

                        NEDRY
              And nobody's going to get hurt. I'm not
              going to have people getting hurt.
@graph.contextPackSummary: SUPER: SAN JOSE, COSTA RICA.

@graph.synopsis: A transport ship plows through rough water.
@graph.scriptText:
12  EXT. OPEN SEA - DAY

    A transport ship plows through rough water.

    SUPER: THREE DAYS LATER.
@graph.contextPackSummary: A transport ship plows through rough water.

@graph.synopsis: A smaller chartered helicopter skims low over the dark water.
@graph.scriptText:
13  EXT. OPEN SEA - DAY

    A smaller chartered helicopter skims low over the dark water.
    On the side: IN-GEN CORPORATION.
@graph.contextPackSummary: A smaller chartered helicopter skims low over the dark water.

@graph.synopsis: Inside. Five passengers. GRANT has his face pressed to the glass,
@graph.scriptText:
14  EXT. HELICOPTER - DAY

    Inside. Five passengers. GRANT has his face pressed to the glass,
    watching the sea below. ELLIE sits next to him, composed. On the
    other side of the cabin: HAMMOND, beaming, impossible to read.

    Beside Hammond: DONALD GENNARO, looking pale with anxiety. And
    next to Gennaro: DR. IAN MALCOLM (fortyish, dressed all in black,
    with snakeskin boots and sunglasses) who is watching everyone else
    with the air of a man greatly entertained.

                        MALCOLM
              So you two dig up dinosaurs?

                        GRANT
              We try to.

                        MALCOLM
              And Ellie — you're a paleobotanist?

                        ELLIE
              That's right.

                        MALCOLM
              (leaning forward)
              Rocks and leaves. I'm... I find myself very
              attracted to extinct systems.

    He smiles at her. Grant notices.

                        GENNARO
              Malcolm, we're here because I asked for
              experts. I just need to know the island
              has adequate safety precautions in place.

                        MALCOLM
              Safety precautions? I'm afraid my calculations
              are not encouraging. Life will not be
              contained. Life breaks free, it expands to
              new territories and crashes through barriers,
              painfully, maybe even dangerously. But, uh...
              well, there it is.

                        HAMMOND
              Ian happens to disagree with our safety
              procedures.

                        MALCOLM
              I disagree with the fundamental premise of
              the park. You've got serious scientists doing
              serious work here, and I'm saying you just
              erased three billion years of evolutionary
              pressure. Presuming to have it all under
              control.

                        GENNARO
              (to Grant and Ellie)
              You've both seen this sort of thing before?

                        GRANT
              We've, ah... seen something like this, yeah.

    Hammond is watching out the window. He smiles.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Inside. Five passengers. GRANT has his face pressed to the glass,

@graph.synopsis: The helicopter sets down on a helipad cut into the jungle. Through
@graph.scriptText:
14A EXT. HELICOPTER LANDING PAD - DAY

    The helicopter sets down on a helipad cut into the jungle. Through
    the windows, they can see lush tropical growth pressing in on all
    sides. WORKERS wait to greet them.

    The passengers file out. The air is thick with heat and jungle
    smell. Ellie immediately bends down and picks up a leaf — it's
    as big as her hand.

                        ELLIE
              This shouldn't be here.

                        GRANT
              What?

                        ELLIE
              (holding the leaf up)
              It's a West Indian lilac. Hippomane mancinella.
              If the herbivores ate this, they'd be
              poisoned.

    Hammond is already walking toward the waiting JEEPS.

                        HAMMOND
              We'll have a look at that. Make a note,
              would you, Donald?

    Gennaro is writing everything down, impressed by his own
    diligence.

    JEEPS wait. The group piles in. They drive off into the jungle.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The helicopter sets down on a helipad cut into the jungle. Through

@graph.synopsis: The jeeps crest a rise and stop. The group gets out.
@graph.scriptText:
15  EXT. HILLTOP - DAY

    The jeeps crest a rise and stop. The group gets out.

    Before them: a VALLEY, lush and impossibly green. And in the
    valley — moving slowly, impossibly large, a BRACHIOSAURUS.

    Then another. And another.

    An entire HERD, drifting across the flats below, moving in slow
    time, their long necks swinging.

    Grant freezes. He cannot speak. He takes off his hat.

    Ellie grabs his arm to steady herself. She can't speak either.

    Malcolm is grinning.

    HAMMOND stands beside them, watching their faces with open delight.

                        HAMMOND
              Welcome... to Jurassic Park.

    Grant turns in a slow circle. In every direction: dinosaurs.

    The brachiosaurs call to one another — a deep, resonant
    thrumming, more beautiful than anything Grant has ever heard.

                        ELLIE
              (barely a whisper)
              They're... they're moving in herds. They do
              move in herds.

                        GRANT
              They move in herds.

    He sits down. Right there in the grass. Ellie sits with him.
    Hammond crouches beside them. They all stare.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              (to himself)
              How did you do this?

                        HAMMOND
              I'll show you.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The jeeps crest a rise and stop. The group gets out.

@graph.synopsis: The jeeps move along a well-maintained road. Everyone stares out
@graph.scriptText:
16  EXT. PARK ROAD - DAY

    The jeeps move along a well-maintained road. Everyone stares out
    the windows. Shapes move in the tree line. Dinosaurs, unhurried.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The jeeps move along a well-maintained road. Everyone stares out

@graph.synopsis: The jeeps arrive at the Visitor's Center compound. A cluster of
@graph.scriptText:
17  EXT. MAIN COMPOUND - DAY

    The jeeps arrive at the Visitor's Center compound. A cluster of
    modern buildings, handsome architecture, set into the hillside.

    Grant is still looking back over his shoulder at the valley.

                        GRANT
              How? How do you do it?

                        HAMMOND
              I'll show you. Come on.


A18 EXT. VISITOR'S CENTER - DAY

    Hammond leads the group toward the entrance.

                        HAMMOND
              We designed the park not just to be safe
              and educational, but... spectacular. Dr. Grant,
              Dr. Sattler — I think you'll find this rather
              a lot more impressive than a computer
              readout of a skeleton.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The jeeps arrive at the Visitor's Center compound. A cluster of

@graph.synopsis: The group enters the main hall. Above them: two complete dinosaur
@graph.scriptText:
18  INT. VISITOR'S CENTER - DAY

    The group enters the main hall. Above them: two complete dinosaur
    SKELETONS — a T-Rex and a Raptor — arch dramatically through
    the space. GRANT looks up at them.

    The lobby is impressive — polished floors, high windows, a gift
    shop (he doesn't look at it), information displays.

                        HAMMOND
              We'll start with the film. Then the lab.
              Then I'll take you to meet some of our
              residents in person.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The group enters the main hall. Above them: two complete dinosaur

@graph.synopsis: A small theater. Plush seats. The group settles in. The lights
@graph.scriptText:
19  INT. SHOW ROOM - DAY

    A small theater. Plush seats. The group settles in. The lights
    go down.

    On screen: JOHN HAMMOND himself, in a white suit, appears
    against a neutral background. He's holding a walking cane.

                        HAMMOND ON SCREEN
              Hello. I'm John Hammond. Welcome to Jurassic
              Park.

    The real Hammond, sitting in the front row, watches himself on
    screen. He is delighted.

                        HAMMOND ON SCREEN (CONT'D)
              Like many of you, I've spent my career
              pursuing a dream that the brightest minds
              in the world told me was impossible. And
              now, here we are.
@graph.contextPackSummary: A small theater. Plush seats. The group settles in. The lights

@graph.synopsis: The film continues. Animated DNA strands spiral across the screen.
@graph.scriptText:
20  INT. SHOW ROOM - CONTINUOUS

    The film continues. Animated DNA strands spiral across the screen.
    A bright, cartoony figure walks into frame —
@graph.contextPackSummary: The film continues. Animated DNA strands spiral across the screen.

@graph.synopsis: MR. DNA — a cartoon character, a strand of DNA with a cheerful
@graph.scriptText:
21  INT. SHOW ROOM - CONTINUOUS

    MR. DNA — a cartoon character, a strand of DNA with a cheerful
    face — addresses the audience directly.

                        MR. DNA
              (on screen)
              Hi, I'm Mr. DNA! Every living thing on
              earth contains DNA. You can find it in every
              cell of every creature that's ever lived.
              And it's the blueprint for life.

    Grant watches with a complicated expression. Malcolm is studying
    the ceiling.

                        MR. DNA (ON SCREEN) (CONT'D)
              But when a creature dies, its DNA breaks
              down over time. Usually, in a few thousand
              years, nothing's left. So how did we get DNA
              from creatures that died sixty-five million
              years ago?

    The animation shows a mosquito landing on a dinosaur and feeding.

                        MR. DNA (ON SCREEN) (CONT'D)
              Mosquitoes! In prehistoric times, these
              ancient blood-suckers drank the blood of
              dinosaurs. Then they got stuck in tree resin.
              The resin hardened over millions of years
              into amber, and trapped inside — the mosquito,
              with the dinosaur blood still in its belly!

    The animation shows amber forming around the mosquito.

                        MR. DNA (ON SCREEN) (CONT'D)
              From that blood we extracted dinosaur DNA!
              But no dinosaur DNA sample is perfect. So
              where do we fill in the gaps?

    Beat.

                        MR. DNA (ON SCREEN) (CONT'D)
              Frog DNA!

    Grant glances at Ellie. She looks back.
@graph.contextPackSummary: MR. DNA — a cartoon character, a strand of DNA with a cheerful

@graph.synopsis: The film runs on. Grant leans toward Ellie.
@graph.scriptText:
22  INT. SHOW ROOM - CONTINUOUS

    The film runs on. Grant leans toward Ellie.

                        GRANT
              (very quietly)
              Does he know that some frogs can change sex?

                        ELLIE
              (equally quiet)
              Under certain environmental pressures...

    They look at each other. They both know where this leads.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The film runs on. Grant leans toward Ellie.

@graph.synopsis: The film concludes. The lights come up.
@graph.scriptText:
23  INT. SHOW ROOM - CONTINUOUS

    The film concludes. The lights come up.

                        HAMMOND
              Shall we?
@graph.contextPackSummary: The film concludes. The lights come up.

@graph.synopsis: Hammond leads the group down a corridor. Through large windows
@graph.scriptText:
24  INT. HALLWAY - DAY

    Hammond leads the group down a corridor. Through large windows
    on one side: a series of labs. Workers in white coats move about.
    Machinery hums.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Hammond leads the group down a corridor. Through large windows

@graph.synopsis: DR. HENRY WU (30s, calm, precise) meets them at the door.
@graph.scriptText:
25  INT. GENETICS LAB - DAY

    DR. HENRY WU (30s, calm, precise) meets them at the door.

                        WU
              Dr. Grant. Dr. Sattler. Welcome to the lab.

                        GRANT
              I... thank you.

    He's looking around with wide eyes. The lab is extraordinary —
    banks of equipment, specimen jars, screens showing rotating
    gene sequences.

                        WU
              We extract from the amber using a process
              that took years to refine. Ninety-two percent
              of the gaps in the genome we're able to fill
              using fragments from other amber samples.
              The remaining eight percent we fill using
              the frog sequences.

                        MALCOLM
              Which frog?

                        WU
              Several species, actually. We've had the
              best results with West African reed frog.
              Hyperolius viridiflavus. Remarkable genome.

                        MALCOLM
              Hmm.

    He says nothing more. But he's making mental notes.

                        ELLIE
              (looking at a specimen)
              And you have complete genomes for all the
              species?

                        WU
              Fourteen so far. We expect to have another
              six within the year.

                        HAMMOND
              (proudly)
              Fourteen! Can you imagine?

                        GRANT
              Only fourteen species are alive on the island?

                        WU
              For now.

    Wu moves to a large incubation unit. Inside: EGGS.

                        WU (CONT'D)
              All fertilized in the lab. All hatched here.
              Every animal on the island was born right
              in this room.

    Grant looks at the eggs. He steps closer.
@graph.contextPackSummary: DR. HENRY WU (30s, calm, precise) meets them at the door.

@graph.synopsis: Every precaution is taken to prevent
@graph.scriptText:
26  INT. GENETICS LAB - CONTINUOUS

                        WU
              Every precaution is taken to prevent
              unauthorized reproduction. All animals
              on the island are engineered female.

                        MALCOLM
              How do you know they're all female?

                        WU
              We control the sex chromosomally.

                        MALCOLM
              But you said you use frog DNA to fill the
              gaps. Some frog species can spontaneously
              change sex from female to male. In a
              single-sex environment...

                        WU
              Yes. We've, ah, considered that possibility.
              But there's no reason the same thing would
              happen here.

                        MALCOLM
              But conditions are never exactly right, are
              they? There, ah, there's your glass half empty.
              Because the wild is unpredictable. Life finds
              a way.

    Wu smiles politely.

                        WU
              Shall we continue?
@graph.contextPackSummary: Every precaution is taken to prevent

@graph.synopsis: They move through the building.
@graph.scriptText:
27  INT. HALLWAY / STAIRS - DAY

    They move through the building.
@graph.contextPackSummary: They move through the building.

@graph.synopsis: Hammond leads them up a staircase. He's walking faster now.
@graph.scriptText:
28  INT. HALLWAY/STAIRS - DAY

    Hammond leads them up a staircase. He's walking faster now.
    His cane taps briskly on the steps.

                        HAMMOND
              Now this is the part I find most exciting.


    INT. HATCHERY / NURSERY - DAY

    They enter a warm, dimly-lit room. Along the walls: more
    incubation units. In the center of the room: a worktable
    where a TECHNICIAN stands watching an EGG. It's cracking.

    They all step closer. Grant can't help himself.

    The egg rocks. A piece of shell falls away. Then another. A
    small, wet, trembling claw appears. Then a snout. Then a head —

    A BABY RAPTOR, the size of a kitten, struggles out of the egg.
    The technician's hands are there to receive it.

    The baby lifts its head and looks at the assembled humans.
    It's perfect. It's terrifying. It's extraordinary.

    The technician offers the hatchling to Grant. He takes it
    without thinking.

    The baby raptor looks up at him. Its eyes are golden.

                        HAMMOND
              We have a reflex here that — there. You see?
              It's a good sign, when they do that.

                        GRANT
              (very quietly)
              It's... a velociraptor.

                        HAMMOND
              Well, you can imagine how we feel about that.

    Malcolm is watching carefully.

                        MALCOLM
              How many raptors?

                        WU
              We have eight. Born this year.

                        MALCOLM
              And the containment?

                        HAMMOND
              We've taken every precaution. You'll see.
              They have a special situation.

                        MALCOLM
              Must be quite a special situation.

    Grant is still holding the hatchling. He strokes it gently
    with one finger. The baby makes a soft sound.

                        ELLIE
              Alan. It's not that bad, is it?

    He looks up.

                        GRANT
              (quietly)
              No. It's... it's not that bad.

    He hands the hatchling back to the technician.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              But I need to see the raptors. The adults.

                        HAMMOND
              Of course.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Hammond leads them up a staircase. He's walking faster now.

@graph.synopsis: A high electric fence encloses a thick area of jungle. Warning
@graph.scriptText:
29  EXT. RAPTOR PEN - DAY

    A high electric fence encloses a thick area of jungle. Warning
    signs line the perimeter. WORKERS observe from a catwalk.
    An electric cargo line hums above the pen.

    MULDOON is there. He sees Grant and extends a hand.

                        MULDOON
              Dr. Grant. Robert Muldoon. I'm the park
              warden.

                        GRANT
              I saw your name in the incident report.
              The worker who was killed —

                        MULDOON
              Yes. She came at the fencing when the pack
              was close. They remember.

    The cargo line hums. A LARGE PIG is lowered into the pen.
    They all watch.

    For a moment: nothing.

    Then — movement in the trees. Fast. The pig is gone. A brief
    chaos of thrashing, a terrible sound —

    Silence.

                        GRANT
              How many are there?

                        MULDOON
              Eight. All female.

                        GRANT
              All females.

                        MULDOON
              They were a mistake. They're lethal at eight
              months and I do not agree with the number
              that Hammond's decided to keep them at.

                        HAMMOND
              (tightly)
              Robert...

                        MULDOON
              They should all be destroyed.

    A long pause.

                        GRANT
              What kind are they?

                        MULDOON
              Velociraptor. Sub-adult, most of them. The
              Big One is fully mature. She's got a rogue
              gene that makes her... bigger than the others.
              And smarter. She worked out how to attack
              the electric fences on three sides at once.
              She'll attack the barrier systematically —
              test for weaknesses, methodically.

    He pauses.

                        MULDOON (CONT'D)
              And she's always watching.

    Grant stares into the trees. Somewhere in there — a golden
    eye stares back.
@graph.contextPackSummary: A high electric fence encloses a thick area of jungle. Warning

@graph.slugline: INT. VISITOR CENTER PRESENTATION ROOM - DAY
@graph.synopsis: Lunch. A long table, fine food, cool air conditioning. Out the
@graph.scriptText:
30  INT. VISITOR CENTER PRESENTATION ROOM - DAY

    Lunch. A long table, fine food, cool air conditioning. Out the
    window: the park in full afternoon sun.

    Hammond is holding court. He's projected several slides —
    visitor projections, revenue charts, happy families eating
    ice cream. He beams at all of it.

    Grant eats. He's not saying much.

    Ellie watches Malcolm, who is watching Hammond with the
    careful attention of a man who has already figured out the
    answer.

                        MALCOLM
              The lack of humility before nature that's
              being displayed here staggers me.

                        HAMMOND
              (mildly)
              Thank you, Ian. Thank you.

                        MALCOLM
              No, no, no. Don't thank me. Don't thank me.
              I'm being very accurate about this. A
              controlled zone in the middle of the Pacific
              Ocean, sixty-five million years of genetic
              evolution kept from happening, and you have
              no idea what you've actually done. You stood
              on the shoulders of geniuses to accomplish
              something as fast as you could, and before
              you even knew what you had, you patented it,
              packaged it, slapped it on a plastic lunchbox,
              and now you're selling it.

                        HAMMOND
              (still pleasant)
              I don't think you're giving us enough credit.
              Our scientists have done things that have
              never been done before.

                        MALCOLM
              Yeah. But your scientists were so preoccupied
              with whether or not they could that they
              didn't stop to think if they should.

    Silence.

                        MALCOLM (CONT'D)
              Genetic power is the most awesome force the
              planet's ever seen, but you wield it like a
              kid that's found his dad's gun.

                        GENNARO
              (uncomfortable)
              Um. I think some points here are... very
              well taken. But there are a few issues that
              will simply have to be —

                        HAMMOND
              (still smiling)
              Donald, the inspector's here. I think that
              if we're going to discuss the merits, we
              should —

    A noise from the lobby. Hammond brightens.

                        HAMMOND (CONT'D)
              Ah. They're here.


A31 INT. VISITOR'S CENTER LOBBY - DAY

    Two CHILDREN wait by the dinosaur skeletons — TIM MURPHY
    (about nine, a boy with wide, serious eyes who is clearly
    obsessed with dinosaurs) and LEX MURPHY (a girl, about twelve,
    with a sharp, challenging face). They're Hammond's grandchildren.

    They spot Hammond.

                        TIM
              Grandpa!

    Hammond swoops them up, one in each arm, or tries to — he gets
    Tim, manages an awkward hug for Lex.

                        HAMMOND
              Tim! Lex! How was the flight?

                        LEX
              Bumpy.

    Hammond introduces them to the group.

                        HAMMOND
              Dr. Grant, Dr. Sattler, Dr. Malcolm — this
              is Tim and Lex. My grandchildren. They'll
              be joining the tour.

    Grant stares at the children.

                        GRANT
              (to Hammond, quietly)
              You'll want to let their parents know —

                        HAMMOND
              Their parents are... it's fine. It's
              absolutely fine. They're very well-behaved.

    Tim has spotted Grant's raptor CLAW, which Grant carries on his
    belt.

                        TIM
              Is that a real raptor claw? Because I've
              read about raptors and I think they're
              the most dangerous dinosaur on the island
              because of their intelligence and hunting
              behavior.

    Grant looks down at him. A long pause.

                        GRANT
              That's correct.

    Tim beams. This is the best day of his life.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Lunch. A long table, fine food, cool air conditioning. Out the

@graph.synopsis: Four FORD EXPLORER XLT tour vehicles wait in front of the
@graph.scriptText:
31  EXT. VISITOR'S CENTER - DAY

    Four FORD EXPLORER XLT tour vehicles wait in front of the
    Visitor's Center. They're painted in Jurassic Park livery,
    and they're electric — guided by an underground rail system
    embedded in the road.

    The vehicles are a self-guided tour. No drivers. State of the
    art video screens inside, interactive displays, touch screens
    on the windows.

    The group assembles. GRANT and ELLIE, MALCOLM and GENNARO.
    HAMMOND stands to one side — he won't be joining the tour.

                        HAMMOND
              I'll monitor from the control room. You'll
              have the full tour experience.

    Tim is already examining the tour vehicles. He slides into
    the back seat of the first car, next to Grant. Lex gets in
    the front. Malcolm settles into the second car with Gennaro.

    Grant looks down at Tim. Tim looks up at Grant with eager
    attention.

                        GRANT
              You ever read my book?

                        TIM
              I read both of your books. I liked the
              first one more.

    Grant blinks.

                        GRANT
              What was wrong with the second one?

                        TIM
              The part about the velociraptor sexual
              dimorphism was incomplete. You couldn't know
              the males were larger because you only had
              three complete specimens.

    A beat.

                        GRANT
              ...Fair point.

    Ellie, at the window, stifles a laugh.

    The cars start up. Silently, smoothly, they begin to move.
    The tour has begun.

    Hammond watches from the doorway, beaming.

    Lex leans out the window.

                        LEX
              Grandpa! He's going to ruin everything!

                        HAMMOND
              (cheerfully)
              Enjoy the tour!

    The vehicles glide away down the road. Grant stares out the
    window, Tim at his side, talking already about the triceratops
    they'll see first.

    Grant's expression: complicated.

===============================================================================
END OF ACT ONE — Scene 31

@graph.synopsis: The nerve center of Jurassic Park. Banks of monitors, radar, a
@graph.scriptText:
32  INT. CONTROL ROOM - DAY

    The nerve center of Jurassic Park. Banks of monitors, radar, a
    weather display. Two men stand at the primary consoles.

    RAY ARNOLD (50s, perpetually cigarette-in-mouth, a chain-smoker
    whose technical competence is not matched by his bedside manner)
    watches a row of screens showing the tour vehicles moving along
    the park road.

    MULDOON stands beside him, arms folded, watching.

    HAMMOND enters behind them, full of cheer.

                        HAMMOND
              How are they doing?

                        ARNOLD
              Moving at six miles per hour. Right on
              schedule.

    Behind him, at a separate console: DENNIS NEDRY. He's eating a
    candy bar, watching his own monitors. He looks up.

                        NEDRY
              I finished the vehicle interface changes you
              wanted, Mr. Hammond. The interactive displays
              work now.

                        HAMMOND
              Wonderful, Dennis. Wonderful.

    Arnold checks a weather display. A dense mass of storm system
    is approaching the island from the west.

                        ARNOLD
              Storm's going to be on us tonight. Tropical
              system. Some wind, some rain. Should be fine
              for the tour.

                        MULDOON
              And if it isn't?

                        ARNOLD
              We bring the cars in early.

                        MULDOON
              Through the paddocks?

                        ARNOLD
              (cigarette bobbing)
              Relax, Robert.

@graph.synopsis: The tour vehicles glide away from the Visitor's Center.
@graph.scriptText:
33  EXT. VISITOR'S CENTER - DAY

    The tour vehicles glide away from the Visitor's Center.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The tour vehicles glide away from the Visitor's Center.

@graph.synopsis: The vehicles pass through the main entrance gates of the park
@graph.scriptText:
33A EXT. MAIN GATES - DAY

    The vehicles pass through the main entrance gates of the park
    proper. Iron gates, the JURASSIC PARK logo arching above them.
    Impressive. A little forbidding.

    Inside the first car, Tim presses his face to the glass.

                        TIM
              Is that electrified?

                        GRANT
              I imagine so.

                        TIM
              Cool.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The vehicles pass through the main entrance gates of the park

@graph.synopsis: The vehicles cruise along the tour road. Ahead: the first exhibit.
@graph.scriptText:
34  EXT. JURASSIC PARK - DAY

    The vehicles cruise along the tour road. Ahead: the first exhibit.
    A sign: DILOPHOSAURUS - THE SPITTER.

    The cars slow and stop. The interactive screens inside light up.
    A cheerful narrator begins —

                        NARRATOR (V.O.)
              Welcome to Jurassic Park! You are now
              approaching the Dilophosaurus paddock.
              The Dilophosaurus was one of the first
              large meat-eating dinosaurs to appear on
              earth, over one hundred and ninety million
              years ago...

    Everyone waits. They scan the treeline.

    Nothing.

                        GENNARO
              (to Malcolm, in the second car)
              Where is it?

                        MALCOLM
              It's a dinosaur, not a movie on demand.

    The cars wait. A full minute. Nothing moves in the paddock.

                        TIM
              (peering)
              I can't see anything.

                        GRANT
              They don't have to come out.

    After another moment, the cars quietly begin to move on.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The vehicles cruise along the tour road. Ahead: the first exhibit.

@graph.synopsis: Arnold watches the tour monitors. He lights a new cigarette off
@graph.scriptText:
35  INT. CONTROL ROOM - DAY

    Arnold watches the tour monitors. He lights a new cigarette off
    the stub of his old one.

                        ARNOLD
              Dilophosaurus is hiding again.

                        HAMMOND
              (behind him)
              She'll come out.

                        ARNOLD
              She never comes out.

    Nedry is at his station. He's opened a new screen — a small
    window showing the internal security camera feeds. He tabs through
    them, pausing at the FERTILIZATION LAB camera.

    He watches the lab for a moment. Makes note of who's there.
    Then closes the window and goes back to eating.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Arnold watches the tour monitors. He lights a new cigarette off

@graph.synopsis: The cars slow and stop. The cars are right next to a concrete
@graph.scriptText:
36  EXT. TYRANNOSAUR PADDOCK - DAY

    The cars slow and stop. The cars are right next to a concrete
    moat — on the far side, a massive fence. Beyond the fence:
    open ground, trees, a meadow.

    And a GOAT. Tethered to a post in the middle of the meadow.

    The goat munches grass, unconcerned.

                        NARRATOR (V.O.)
              You are now approaching the Tyrannosaur
              paddock — home to the largest predator
              in the history of the earth. The Tyrannosaurus
              Rex was first described by Henry Fairfield
              Osborn in nineteen-oh-five...

    Tim has his face pressed to the window. His eyes are saucer-wide.

                        TIM
              It eats the goat?

    Grant doesn't answer. He's watching.

    The goat munches. The meadow is still.

    Malcolm, in the second car, checks his watch. He glances at
    Ellie.

                        MALCOLM
              The rex doesn't show itself on schedule.
              Typical.

                        ELLIE
              Typical?

                        MALCOLM
              (tilting head)
              Chaotically typical. There is no schedule
              for a Tyrannosaurus Rex. The whole idea of
              predictable dinosaur behavior is inherently —

    He gestures: absurd.

    The goat continues to eat.

    Gennaro is checking his clipboard. He's writing notes.

                        GENNARO
              (muttering)
              When does it normally appear?

                        GRANT
              (calling back)
              It's a dinosaur, Gennaro. Not a nature show.

    After a long wait: nothing. The meadow stays empty. The goat
    is unbothered.

    The cars begin to move again. The goat watches them leave.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              (to Tim, quietly)
              Don't worry. There'll be other chances.

                        TIM
              Do you think it was watching us? From inside
              the trees?

    Grant considers this.

                        GRANT
              Probably.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The cars slow and stop. The cars are right next to a concrete

@graph.synopsis: Arnold and Hammond watch the monitor showing the tour vehicles.
@graph.scriptText:
37  INT. CONTROL ROOM - DAY

    Arnold and Hammond watch the monitor showing the tour vehicles.

                        ARNOLD
              T-Rex didn't show.

                        HAMMOND
              She'll get hungry. She'll show tonight.

                        ARNOLD
              By tonight we'll have called them back in
              because of the storm.

                        HAMMOND
              Well, then she'll show next week for the
              next tour.

    Arnold stubs out his cigarette.

                        ARNOLD
              You know, if you'd just let me put the
              feeding schedule back to twice weekly instead
              of — never mind. Never mind.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Arnold and Hammond watch the monitor showing the tour vehicles.

@graph.synopsis: The tour vehicles cruise along the road. Malcolm has leaned out
@graph.scriptText:
38  EXT. PARK - DAY

    The tour vehicles cruise along the road. Malcolm has leaned out
    his window slightly, his dark hair blowing. He's holding a glass
    of water — from somewhere, he's produced a glass of water.

    The second car slows. ELLIE looks out.

                        ELLIE
              What are you doing?

                        MALCOLM
              (leaning toward her window)
              I'm going to show you something. A small
              demonstration of, uh, chaos theory. Give
              me your hand.

    He extends his own hand, palm up. She hesitates, then extends
    hers. He places her hand flat, turns it over slightly.

                        MALCOLM (CONT'D)
              A drop of water falls on your hand, and
              where does it go? Same place every time?

    He raises his glass, tilts it slightly. A drop falls on the
    back of her hand.

                        ELLIE
              The same place.

                        MALCOLM
              Now.

    He takes her hand gently, tilts it slightly more.

                        MALCOLM (CONT'D)
              Same drop of water falls, same place. Same
              hand. Does it go the same way?

    He tilts the glass again. Another drop. It runs a completely
    different direction.

                        MALCOLM (CONT'D)
              Tiny variations. The butterfly effect. At
              the beginning, we can't know which way the
              drop will run. It's unpredictable. The future
              is unpredictable. The behavior of this system
              is unpredictable. This park is unpredictable.

    Ellie is looking at her hand. He still has hold of it.

                        ELLIE
              That was... very smooth.

                        MALCOLM
              (releasing her hand)
              I'm just pointing out an observation.

    In the first car: GRANT has spotted something in a field to the
    left. He's already halfway out the window.

                        GRANT
              Stop the car!

    The car slows.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              Stop the car!

    He pushes the door open and JUMPS OUT while the car is still
    rolling.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The tour vehicles cruise along the road. Malcolm has leaned out

@graph.synopsis: Arnold sees it on his monitor.
@graph.scriptText:
39  INT. CONTROL ROOM - DAY

    Arnold sees it on his monitor.

                        ARNOLD
              What the — did he just jump out of the car?

    Hammond squints at the screen.

                        HAMMOND
              ...Apparently.

    Nedry, behind them, barely glances up. He's at his console,
    doing something with a code window. He opens a small sub-window —
    the EMBRYO STORAGE UNIT camera — and watches it for a moment.
    Three seconds. Then closes it.

    He opens a text editor and begins to type code.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Arnold sees it on his monitor.

@graph.synopsis: Grant jogs across an open field. The rest of the group pours
@graph.scriptText:
40  EXT. PARK - DAY

    Grant jogs across an open field. The rest of the group pours
    out of the cars after him.

    Ahead: a TRICERATOPS. Enormous, magnificent, lying on its side
    in the field. Being attended to by DR. GERRY HARDING (50s,
    quiet, utterly at home in the presence of enormous animals).

                        HARDING
              (without looking up)
              Keep your voices down. She's been sick
              for six weeks and we haven't found the
              cause.

    They approach slowly. The triceratops breathes — slow, deep
    breaths. Up close she's extraordinary. Her frill is faded,
    dull.

    ELLIE is at her side instantly, professional mode.

                        ELLIE
              What are her symptoms?

                        HARDING
              Disorientation, loss of balance. Happened
              again this afternoon — that's what we got
              the call on.

    Ellie kneels down. Her hands move over the triceratops. She's
    looking at the animal's tongue, her pupils.

                        ELLIE
              How often does this happen?

                        HARDING
              Weekly. Sometimes twice a week. Always in
              the late afternoon.

    Grant crouches near the animal's massive head. He reaches out
    and places his hand on her nose. She doesn't react.

                        GRANT
              She's something.

                        TIM
              She's beautiful. She's the most beautiful
              thing I've ever seen.

    Even Gennaro looks moved, briefly.

                        MALCOLM
              (watching Ellie)
              I, uh, also find this view rather extraordinary.

    He's not looking at the triceratops.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Grant jogs across an open field. The rest of the group pours

@graph.synopsis: The radar shows the storm system has accelerated. Arnold checks
@graph.scriptText:
41A INT. CONTROL ROOM - DAY

    The radar shows the storm system has accelerated. Arnold checks
    the map.

                        ARNOLD
              Storm's picked up. It's going to hit tonight,
              not tomorrow morning.

                        MULDOON
              We need to bring them back now.

                        ARNOLD
              (reaching for the radio)
              Yeah. Yeah, you're right.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The radar shows the storm system has accelerated. Arnold checks

@graph.slugline: INT. CONTROL ROOM - ACROSS THE ROOM
@graph.synopsis: At Nedry's console, the screen shows a docked chat window.
@graph.scriptText:
41  INT. CONTROL ROOM - ACROSS THE ROOM

    At Nedry's console, the screen shows a docked chat window.
    Text appears: 'BOAT WILL WAIT TWO HOURS ONLY. NOT LONGER.'

    Nedry reads it. Looks at the clock. Types back: 'WILL BE THERE.'

    He closes the window.
@graph.contextPackSummary: At Nedry's console, the screen shows a docked chat window.

@graph.synopsis: Ellie examines the droppings near the triceratops. They're massive.
@graph.scriptText:
42  EXT. FIELD - DAY

    Ellie examines the droppings near the triceratops. They're massive.
    She's poking at them, unselfconsciously.

                        HARDING
              She eats West Indian lilac sometimes —
              the plant grows on the far side of the paddock.
              We never thought she was eating enough to
              cause harm.

    Ellie looks up.

                        ELLIE
              You've been looking in the wrong place.

    She holds up a handful of small stones from the dung pile.

                        ELLIE (CONT'D)
              She swallows them. Gizzard stones — for
              grinding food. Like modern birds. And they
              come from the same area where the lilac grows.
              So every six weeks or so, she replaces her
              gizzard stones —

                        HARDING
              And she ingests the West Indian lilac berries
              along with them.

                        ELLIE
              Six weeks apart. Just like her episodes.

    Malcolm watches Ellie from a distance.

                        MALCOLM
              (to Gennaro)
              She's extraordinary.

    Gennaro looks up from his clipboard.

                        GENNARO
              Who?

                        MALCOLM
              The triceratops.

    He says this without convincing anyone.

    A radio on Harding's belt crackles.

                        ARNOLD (OVER RADIO)
              Dr. Harding, the tour vehicles need to come
              in. Storm's been upgraded. Tour's being cut
              short.

                        HARDING
              Copy that.

    He looks at the group.

                        HARDING (CONT'D)
              You'll have to go back.

                        ELLIE
              Five minutes?

                        HARDING
              Better make it two.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Ellie examines the droppings near the triceratops. They're massive.

@graph.synopsis: Arnold radios the tour vehicles. Behind him, Nedry is at his
@graph.scriptText:
43  INT. CONTROL ROOM - DAY

    Arnold radios the tour vehicles. Behind him, Nedry is at his
    console. He's opened a command window. A large block of code
    is visible.

    At the top of the window: a countdown timer. It's been set to
    18:00 and is holding.

    Nedry looks around. Hammond is at the back of the room on the
    phone. Arnold is looking at the storm display. Muldoon has
    stepped out.

    Nedry looks at the code. His hand hovers over the keyboard.

    Then he puts the candy bar wrapper down and types a single
    command.

                        NEDRY
              (quietly, to himself)
              Say goodbye to the dinosaurs. Goodbye.

    He types: EXECUTE AUTORUN/RAY ARNOLD IS NOT MY SUPERVISOR.

    He hits enter.

    The countdown timer begins: 18:00. 17:59. 17:58.

    On his screen: access protocols begin to fall one by one.
    A list of systems begins to scroll as they go offline.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Arnold radios the tour vehicles. Behind him, Nedry is at his

@graph.synopsis: Tim has found the gizzard stones. He's picking through them
@graph.scriptText:
44  EXT. FIELD - DAY

    Tim has found the gizzard stones. He's picking through them
    carefully.

                        TIM
              What are these ones?

    He holds one up for Ellie.

                        ELLIE
              That's a good one. That's volcanic basalt.
              Where did you find it?

    Tim points. Ellie looks. She exchanges a glance with Grant.

                        ELLIE (CONT'D)
              She's been eating from a very specific spot.
              Right here in the paddock. Smart girl.

                        GRANT
              (to Tim)
              Good find.

    Tim beams.

    The radio crackles again.

                        ARNOLD (OVER RADIO)
              Let's go, people. Now.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Tim has found the gizzard stones. He's picking through them

@graph.synopsis: They head back to the tour vehicles. The sky to the west has
@graph.scriptText:
45  EXT. FIELD - CONTINUOUS

    They head back to the tour vehicles. The sky to the west has
    darkened. You can feel the pressure change.
@graph.contextPackSummary: They head back to the tour vehicles. The sky to the west has

@graph.synopsis: The vehicles head back along the tour road. The sky is darkening
@graph.scriptText:
46  EXT. PARK - DUSK

    The vehicles head back along the tour road. The sky is darkening
    fast. The first raindrops begin to fall. Through the trees,
    the park looks different — less friendly.

    In car one: Grant watches the jungle go by. The rain picks up.

                        TIM
              (pressed to the window)
              I wonder if they go inside when it rains.

                        GRANT
              I don't imagine they have a preference.

                        TIM
              I bet the T-Rex is out in this.

    He says it like that's a good thing. Grant almost smiles.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The vehicles head back along the tour road. The sky is darkening

@graph.synopsis: Arnold watches the cars on the monitors. The storm display shows
@graph.scriptText:
47  INT. CONTROL ROOM - DUSK

    Arnold watches the cars on the monitors. The storm display shows
    the system right on top of them now.

                        ARNOLD
              They're ten minutes out.

    He turns and finds —

    NEDRY IS GONE.

    His console: a screensaver has kicked in. One of those wacky
    screensavers that was fashionable in the early '90s, colorful
    and spinning.

    Arnold squints.

                        ARNOLD (CONT'D)
              Dennis?

    No answer.

                        ARNOLD (CONT'D)
              (to no one)
              Hm.

    He goes back to the storm display. Behind him, on the row of
    monitors showing park security cameras — one by one, silently,
    screens are going dark.


    EXT. VISITOR'S CENTER - DUSK

    Nedry has crossed the parking area to a STAFF JEEP. Number 12.
    He checks his watch — the Barbasol can in one hand. He's
    already moving fast.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Arnold watches the cars on the monitors. The storm display shows

@graph.synopsis: The tour vehicles move through heavy rain. Headlights cut through
@graph.scriptText:
48  EXT. PARK ROAD - NIGHT

    The tour vehicles move through heavy rain. Headlights cut through
    the darkness. Inside car one, Grant watches the rain and the
    road.

    Malcolm is in car two with Gennaro. Malcolm is talking. Gennaro
    is writing.

    In car one:

                        TIM
              (looking at the monitor in the car)
              How come the video feed isn't working?

    The screen is dark. Static.

                        GRANT
              I don't know.

    He looks at the control panel. Several systems appear to be dark.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              (to himself)
              Something's wrong.

    He picks up the car's intercom.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              Hello? Control? Can anybody hear me?

    No answer.

    In car two:

                        MALCOLM
              (mid-lecture)
              — at the fractal edge of complex systems,
              you get this recurring motif. Self-similarity.
              The shape of the coastline is the same at
              a kilometer scale and at a meter scale. The
              shape of a leaf reflects the tree that bears
              it. This is what we're dealing with in an
              ecosystem, and to imagine that we can simply
              insert — 

    He pauses. Something's wrong with the car. It's slowing.

                        MALCOLM (CONT'D)
              Why are we slowing?

                        GENNARO
              I don't know. Where's the control room?
              They should be —

    The car stops. The road is dark. The cars have stopped right
    at the T-Rex paddock.

    Beyond the fence: the goat's tether. The goat is gone.

    Malcolm and Gennaro notice at the same moment.

                        GENNARO
              Where's the... where's the goat?

    Silence but for the rain.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The tour vehicles move through heavy rain. Headlights cut through

@graph.synopsis: Dark. Nedry moves quickly through the lab with a penlight,
@graph.scriptText:
49  INT. FERTILIZATION LAB - NIGHT

    Dark. Nedry moves quickly through the lab with a penlight,
    heading for the EMBRYO STORAGE UNIT — a tall refrigerated
    cylinder against the wall.

    He works the combination. The door hisses open — cold vapor
    pours out. Inside: racks of small labeled vials. One for every
    species.

    He reads the labels in his penlight beam, one by one.

    He opens the Barbasol can. Inside: cryo-cells, ready.

    He begins to transfer embryos.

                        NEDRY
              (reading labels, muttering)
              Brachiosaurus... Stegosaurus... Triceratops...
              Dilophosaurus... T-Rex...

    He works quickly and methodically. It doesn't take long.

    He closes the can. Closes the storage unit. Looks around.

    He heads for the door.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Dark. Nedry moves quickly through the lab with a penlight,

@graph.synopsis: Arnold stares at his screens. The monitors are wrong. Readings
@graph.scriptText:
50  INT. CONTROL ROOM - NIGHT

    Arnold stares at his screens. The monitors are wrong. Readings
    are wrong. He reaches for his radio.

                        ARNOLD
              Muldoon. Come to the control room.

    He begins to type. Something is very wrong with the system.

                        ARNOLD (CONT'D)
              (to himself)
              Nedry. What did you do?
@graph.contextPackSummary: Arnold stares at his screens. The monitors are wrong. Readings

@graph.synopsis: Nedry races to Jeep 12 in the pouring rain. He jumps in, starts
@graph.scriptText:
51  EXT. VISITOR'S CENTER - NIGHT

    Nedry races to Jeep 12 in the pouring rain. He jumps in, starts
    the engine, and tears out of the compound.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Nedry races to Jeep 12 in the pouring rain. He jumps in, starts

@graph.synopsis: Muldoon enters. Arnold is working fast.
@graph.scriptText:
52  INT. CONTROL ROOM - NIGHT

    Muldoon enters. Arnold is working fast.

                        MULDOON
              What's wrong?

                        ARNOLD
              Nedry's shut down the security systems.
              I can't reach the tour vehicles, I can't
              access the paddock controls —

    He types. Nothing.

                        ARNOLD (CONT'D)
              He's hidden it behind some kind of password
              protection. A whole system patch.

                        HAMMOND
              (appearing in the doorway)
              What's happening?

                        ARNOLD
              Security systems are failing across the park.
              The fences —

    He stares at a readout.

                        ARNOLD (CONT'D)
              The fences are off.

    A silence.

                        MULDOON
              All of them?

                        ARNOLD
              Not all. The land fences are still partially
              on. But the T-Rex paddock —

                        HAMMOND
              Turn them back on!

                        ARNOLD
              I can't! He's blocked my access!
@graph.contextPackSummary: Muldoon enters. Arnold is working fast.

@graph.synopsis: The two tour vehicles are stopped side by side on the road
@graph.scriptText:
53  EXT. TYRANNOSAUR PADDOCK - NIGHT

    The two tour vehicles are stopped side by side on the road
    beside the paddock.

    In car one: GRANT. TIM. LEX. Rain hammers the roof.

    In car two: MALCOLM. GENNARO.

    Nobody moves.

    Tim reaches forward and picks up a pair of NIGHT VISION GOGGLES
    from the glovebox — souvenir-style ones, but apparently functional.
    He puts them on and peers through the window.

                        TIM
              I can see everything in the green.

    He sweeps the goggles across the paddock.

    The fence.

    The fence is DOWN. Not electrified. The wires hang loose and
    dark.

                        TIM (CONT'D)
              The fence isn't on anymore.

                        GRANT
              What?

                        TIM
              (goggles still on)
              The lights that show it's on — they're off.

    A pause.

                        GRANT
              Take those off for a second and give them
              to me.

    He takes the goggles. Looks. The fence is dark.

    He looks at the road ahead. The rain is very heavy now. Hard
    to see anything beyond a few yards.

    But then: a RIPPLE in a puddle on the road. A vibration.
    Then another. Regular. Getting stronger.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              (very quietly)
              Everybody... be absolutely still.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The two tour vehicles are stopped side by side on the road

@graph.synopsis: In car two: MALCOLM sees the ripples too. He goes completely still.
@graph.scriptText:
54  EXT. TYRANNOSAUR PADDOCK - CONTINUOUS

    In car two: MALCOLM sees the ripples too. He goes completely still.
    He is staring at a puddle on the dashboard where water has collected.
    The puddle is shaking. Rhythmically.

                        MALCOLM
              (barely audible)
              Oh no. Oh no no no.
@graph.contextPackSummary: In car two: MALCOLM sees the ripples too. He goes completely still.

@graph.synopsis: From the darkness on the far side of the fence: a sound. A
@graph.scriptText:
55  EXT. TYRANNOSAUR PADDOCK - CONTINUOUS

    From the darkness on the far side of the fence: a sound. A
    low, rumbling exhalation. Like distant thunder, but too regular.

    Then: a foot appears. Monstrous. Taloned. It lands with
    tremendous deliberateness on the road.

    Then another foot.

    The T-REX steps into the headlights.

    She is enormous. She is beyond enormous. She stands perhaps
    twenty feet at the shoulder, with a head the size of a car.
    Rain streams off her scales. Her breath makes clouds in the
    wet air.

    She looks at the vehicles.

    Grant is not breathing.

    Tim is not breathing.

    Lex's hand finds Grant's arm and grips it.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              (almost inaudible)
              Don't move. Don't move a muscle.
@graph.contextPackSummary: From the darkness on the far side of the fence: a sound. A

@graph.synopsis: The T-Rex moves toward the vehicles. Her footfalls shake the
@graph.scriptText:
56  INT. CAR ONE - CONTINUOUS

    The T-Rex moves toward the vehicles. Her footfalls shake the
    car. She bends her head toward car one and sniffs.

    Lex makes a sound — almost a whimper.

                        GRANT
              Shhhh.

    The T-Rex's eye — enormous, golden, completely alien — swings
    to the window. She is LOOKING RIGHT AT THEM.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              Her vision is based on movement. Like a frog.
              If we don't move —

    The T-Rex tilts her head. Still looking.

    And then — in car two — GENNARO panics.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The T-Rex moves toward the vehicles. Her footfalls shake the

@graph.synopsis: Gennaro throws the car door open and runs. Right into the rain.
@graph.scriptText:
57  INT. CAR TWO - CONTINUOUS

    Gennaro throws the car door open and runs. Right into the rain.
    He sprints for the porta-potty shelter at the edge of the
    paddock.

    Malcolm watches in disbelief.

                        MALCOLM
              When you gotta go, you gotta go.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Gennaro throws the car door open and runs. Right into the rain.

@graph.synopsis: The T-Rex's head swings toward the fleeing GENNARO. Her focus
@graph.scriptText:
58  EXT. TYRANNOSAUR PADDOCK - CONTINUOUS

    The T-Rex's head swings toward the fleeing GENNARO. Her focus
    has shifted entirely. She's tracking movement.

    She steps toward car two.

    Malcolm grabs the door handle — and the T-Rex's foot comes down
    on the roof of car two, crushing it slightly. Malcolm gets
    the door open and rolls out, landing hard on the road.

                        MALCOLM
              Dammit! Run! Ruuuun!

    He lurches to his feet and half-limps, half-runs.

    The T-Rex picks up car two in her jaws. Shakes it. Drops it.

    In car one: Grant has found a ROAD FLARE. He lights it. Jabs it
    out the window at the T-Rex. She turns her head, momentarily
    distracted.

                        TIM
              What are you doing?!

                        GRANT
              Creating a distraction!

    He throws the flare. It bounces, sparking, into the paddock.
    The T-Rex watches it, head tilted.

    Grant throws open the car door.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              Get out! Both of you! Now!

    He pushes Lex out first, then Tim. They stumble into the rain.

    The T-Rex turns back toward them. She's right there. She's
    RIGHT THERE.
@graph.contextPackSummary: The T-Rex's head swings toward the fleeing GENNARO. Her focus

@graph.synopsis: Grant hustles the kids along the concrete barrier at the edge
@graph.scriptText:
59  EXT. TYRANNOSAUR PADDOCK - CONTINUOUS

    Grant hustles the kids along the concrete barrier at the edge
    of the paddock. They press against the wall, moving fast.

    The T-Rex follows the edge of the road. Her head sweeps over
    them. She is oriented by sound and movement. They try not to
    make either.

    Car one is still on the road. The T-Rex approaches it and
    shoves it. The car rolls, tips —

    Then topples over the CONCRETE BARRIER at the edge of the
    paddock. The car falls, tumbles, drops out of sight.

    The impact shakes the ground.
@graph.contextPackSummary: Grant hustles the kids along the concrete barrier at the edge

@graph.synopsis: Malcolm drags himself along the barrier. His leg is bad —
@graph.scriptText:
60  EXT. TYRANNOSAUR PADDOCK - CONTINUOUS

    Malcolm drags himself along the barrier. His leg is bad —
    he took a hit when he rolled from the car. He's limping hard.

    The T-Rex is moving along the far side of the barrier, tracking.

    Grant looks back — the kids are with him, pressed against the
    fence. He looks up. The barrier edge. Eight feet of concrete.
    Below: the paddock. Darkness.

    He makes a decision: he grabs a cable running along the fence —
    a safety cable — and throws Lex's hands onto it.

                        GRANT
              Hold on! Don't let go!

    She grabs it. He grabs Tim.

                        GRANT (CONT'D)
              Hold onto me and close your eyes.

    The T-Rex swings back toward them. Her foot crashes down ten
    feet away. The wall shudders.

    Grant, holding Tim, swings out over the barrier on the cable
    and begins to descend — hand over hand — into the darkness of
    the paddock below.

    Lex follows.

    Above them: the T-Rex appears at the barrier edge. She looks
    down at the dangling figures. She can't quite reach.

    She bellows. The sound shakes the cliff face.

    Grant and the kids hit the ground and run.

===============================================================================
END OF ACT TWO-A — Scene 60
